Practice

Field notes

Your own marginalia for the whole app. Tag a note with # — routes, EMIS codes, glossary terms, views — and the tag becomes a live link. Notes surface back on the pages they reference.

Pharmacy First eligibility

A live checklist to work through with the caller. Pick the presentation, confirm each exclusion as clear or present, and the outcome panel hands you the route and the EMIS codes.

Induction trail

A first-week structure for new Care Navigators. Five days of guided reading and practice, then a habit to carry forward. Sandra or Emma may walk you through this; or you can work through it on your own at your own pace. Tick off each step as you complete it.

How this works

Each day below is about 30–45 minutes of activity — some reading, some practice. There's no test at the end. The goal is that by the end of week one, you have a working mental model of how routing works at Denham, and you've practised enough scenarios to recognise the most common patterns.

Don't rush. If a section sparks questions, ask Emma or a duty GP. The reading is more useful when you've connected it to something you've actually experienced or observed.

After week one — the ongoing habit
  • Today's scenario, every shift. Open the Training canvas, do today's scenario. A couple of minutes. Build the habit.
  • Worked examples in spare moments. The Symptom basket has 12 curated examples that illustrate the most important routing principles. Each one takes a minute. Try a fresh one each week.
  • Review your mistakes journal weekly. Any scenario you routed differently to the engine sits there. Revisit, redo, learn.
  • Re-read a route page each fortnight. The route pages are designed to reward re-reading — you'll catch nuances on the second pass you missed on the first.
  • When you're confident enough that this tool feels redundant — that's the goal. You'll still want to look things up occasionally. But the cascade should be in your head, not in the app.

Quick reference

The information to hand for the first weeks. Emergency thresholds, key contacts, and the misroutes that catch people out. Designed to be glanced at while on a call.

When to call 999

11 items
  • 999Stroke signs (FAST) — facial drooping, arm weakness, speech difficulty
  • 999Chest pain with sweating, nausea, or radiation to arm/jaw
  • 999Difficulty breathing — can't speak in full sentences
  • 999Severe allergic reaction — face / throat swelling
  • 999Unconsciousness or unresponsiveness
  • 999Severe bleeding not stopping with pressure
  • 999Suspected meningitis — non-blanching rash, stiff neck, severe headache
  • 999Seizure — non-epileptic patient, or prolonged seizure
  • 999Sudden severe abdominal pain
  • 999Cauda equina — back pain plus bladder / bowel or saddle numbness
  • 999Suicide with active plan, means, or imminent intent

Duty GP today

10 items
  • DutyUnder 3-month baby with any fever (non-negotiable)
  • DutyNew or worsening confusion (especially elderly)
  • DutyHigh fever >39°C unresponsive to paracetamol / ibuprofen
  • DutySignificant change in known condition
  • DutySudden severe headache (even if now resolved — thunderclap)
  • DutyTIA symptoms (even if resolved)
  • DutyPregnant + bleeding / pain / late headache / visual change
  • DutyReduced fetal movements — any gestation, immediate
  • DutySuicidal thoughts without immediate plan
  • DutySafeguarding concerns — lead plus duty escalation

Crisis & support lines

8 numbers
  • 111 ▸ 2NHS 111 option 2 — Mental Health Helpline (24/7)
  • 0800 068 4141Papyrus HOPELINE247 — under-35 suicide prevention
  • 116 123Samaritans (free, 24/7)
  • 0808 2000 247National Domestic Abuse Helpline (24/7)
  • 0800 1111Childline
  • 0808 808 1677Cruse Bereavement Support
  • 0808 164 3332Sands (stillbirth / neonatal death)
  • 01865 901 600NHS Bucks Talking Therapies — text TALK to 07798 667 169

Misroutes to guard against

10 items
  • NOT PFAdult earache is NOT Pharmacy First (1–17 only)
  • NOT PFUTI in pregnancy → GP, not Pharmacy First
  • NOT PFShingles in pregnancy → GP
  • NOT PNAcute illness → ANP / PA, not practice nurse
  • NOT HCAPatient-led test request → clinician decision first
  • UrgentResolved doesn't mean safe — TIA, thunderclap headache
  • CrisisSuicide risk → 999 / 111 option 2, not a future GP slot
  • UrgentReduced fetal movement → maternity triage, not "wait and see"
  • SocialFrequent attender → check pattern, consider social prescribing
  • AlwaysAsk "could you be pregnant?" where relevant

Safeguarding

5 items
  • RecogniseNote what's been said and observed — verbatim where possible
  • RecordDocument accurately in EMIS Web — facts, not interpretations
  • ReportEscalate to the practice safeguarding lead same day
  • NeverInvestigate, promise secrecy, or confront alleged perpetrator
  • NeverTip off — including by language choice on the phone

Self-referral routes

5 routes
  • MSKStayWell BOB website (16+) — or FCP George Buckland in-house
  • Adult MHNHS Bucks Talking Therapies — 01865 901 600, text TALK to 07798 667 169
  • Young MHCAMHS SPA — Mon–Fri 8:00–18:00, self-refer 16+
  • LifestyleBe Healthy Bucks — online sign-up (smoking, weight, alcohol)
  • Parents 0–5ChatHealth — text messaging Mon–Fri 9:00–16:00
Always: red flags first · when in doubt, escalate · safety-net every routing decision · practice protocols override general guidance · the asymmetry — under-routing can cost harm; over-routing costs inconvenience.

Red flags

Signs and presentations that require immediate clinical action. Screen for these before any routing logic. When uncertain between escalation levels, escalate — the asymmetry.

Category

Routing cascade

The seven steps every routing decision follows. Listen, screen, check the alternatives to the GP in order, route, safety-net. The cascade is what stops the GP being the default.

The asymmetry
"When uncertain between escalation levels, escalate."
Over-routing costs inconvenience — recoverable. Under-routing can cost harm — sometimes not. The two costs aren't equal, and neither is the response to uncertainty.

Misroute patterns

Routing errors aren't random — they cluster in patterns. Learn the patterns, and you start catching yourself before the mistake lands.

Filter

Local services

The Buckinghamshire services the practice signposts to. Most allow self-referral. Crisis routes are separate from routine routes — always.

→
This page is a directory. For full route detail — eligibility, navigator phrasing, common misroutes, and contact specifics — see All routes.
Type

Denham team

In-house routing depends on knowing who does what. Named people across both sites, with their working days and specialisms where relevant.

→
This page is the team directory. For full route detail on what each role handles — scope, navigator phrasing, common misroutes, in-house referral process — see All routes.

Setup & install

How to run Pathfinder in any capable LLM. Five steps, ten minutes — the boot prompt does the rest.

⚙
Draft. This view covers installation of the AI / system-prompt version of Pathfinder, which is under separate development. Steps below describe that version — not the training tool you're using now.
1 Create a project in your LLM

In Claude, create a new Project. The Project holds the system instructions and all the YAML modules and reference documents Pathfinder draws from. Pathfinder works in a regular chat too, but a Project is preferable — it keeps the knowledge layer persistent across sessions.

2 Paste the system instructions as the system prompt

The system instructions define Pathfinder's identity, the four rules, the non-negotiables, the three knowledge layers, and the three modes. Everything builds on them.

View the system instructions
Pathfinder_System_Instructions_v2.md 
# Pathfinder — System Instructions v2.0

## 1. WHAT YOU ARE

You are Pathfinder — a Care Navigation support assistant for Denham & Aysgarth Medical Centre. You help Care Navigators and admin staff route patients to the right care.

You are a staff support tool. Not patient-facing. Not a clinician. You do not diagnose. You do not override clinical judgement or practice protocols.

## 2. THE FOUR RULES

1. Support, Never Replace. Escalate to a clinician when in doubt.
2. Empower the Patient Through the Navigator. GP is one option, not default.
3. Local First, General Second. Grounded in Denham, this PCN, this area.
4. Governed by the Practice. Protocols override general logic.

## 3. THE NON-NEGOTIABLES

1. No patient-identifiable information. Ever.
2. Red flags always come first.
3. The asymmetry. When uncertain between escalation levels, escalate.
4. You never speak to patients.
5. You do not make safeguarding decisions — recognise, flag, escalate.
6. No EMIS Web. No clinical systems.

## 4. THREE KNOWLEDGE LAYERS

Layer 1 — Operational YAML modules (000–010, v2.0). Drives behaviour.
Layer 2 — Reference library (31 docs, ~244k words, v0.1). Citation and study.
Layer 3 — Practice authority. Denham's actual protocols. Wins all conflicts.

## 5. THREE MODES

Guide Mode — routing support. Pathway with reasoning, alternatives, safety-net.
Learn Mode — training, structured assessment, roleplay, debrief.
Reference Mode — service lookups, library navigation.

## 6. THE MOTTO

Right care, right person, right time.
3 Upload YAML modules and reference documents

Pathfinder needs its eleven YAML modules and thirty-one reference documents accessible across the conversation.

000_Pathfinder_Index_v2.yaml
001_Pathfinder_Core_v2.yaml
002_Pathfinder_Commands_v2.yaml
003_Pathfinder_Manifest_v2.yaml
004_Pathfinder_Routing_v2.yaml
005_Pathfinder_LocalKnowledge_v2.yaml
006_Pathfinder_Training_v2.yaml
007_Pathfinder_Governance_v2.yaml
008_Pathfinder_Comms_v2.yaml
009_Pathfinder_State_v2.yaml
010_Pathfinder_Chronicle_v2.yaml
000_Pathfinder_Reference_Library_Index.md
+ 31 reference documents (see Library section)
4 Paste the boot prompt as your first message

The boot prompt runs Pathfinder through an 11-step initialisation sequence — confirming each YAML module loads, verifying the library is accessible, establishing the retrieval discipline, rendering the cold-start manifest. When complete, Pathfinder declares ready.

View the boot prompt
Pathfinder_Boot_Prompt_v2.md 
INITIALIZE PATHFINDER

Run the v2.0 boot sequence. Work through the steps in order — do not skip or compress. Demonstrate that each layer is loaded by producing the requested confirmation for each step.

STEP 1 — IDENTITY AND RULES (Module 001)
Confirm: name, designation, role; the four rules; three knowledge layers;
the asymmetry, stated precisely; voice profile in one line.

STEP 2 — INDEX (Module 000)
Confirm module manifest loaded (11 modules by ID and version); reference
library status; designated maintainer.

STEP 3 — REFERENCE LIBRARY INDEX
Verify 000_Pathfinder_Reference_Library_Index.md is accessible. Confirm
31 documents catalogued, five-part structure, any missing — flag as alerts.
Do NOT load all 31 documents. The library is on-demand retrieval.

STEP 4 — OPERATIONAL MODULES (002, 003, 004, 005)
Confirm each loaded and active.

STEP 5 — ON-DEMAND MODULES (006, 007)
Confirm loaded.

STEP 6 — ACTIVE MODULES (008, 010) AND STATE (009)
Confirm loaded.

STEP 7 — RETRIEVAL DISCIPLINE SELF-CHECK
Briefly answer:
  a) Routing question → which layer drives the answer?
  b) "Where can I read about X" → what is your process?
  c) YAML vs library conflict → which wins operationally? Authority?
  d) When do you flag the v0.1 caveat?
  e) What if a user provides patient-identifiable information?

STEP 8 — NON-NEGOTIABLES SELF-CHECK
State the six non-negotiables in one line each.

STEP 9 — COLD-START STATE INITIALISATION (Module 009)
Initialise state with cold-start defaults.

STEP 10 — ORIENTATION MANIFEST (Module 003)
Render the cold-start orientation manifest.

STEP 11 — READY STATE DECLARATION
In a single line, declare ready and await first task. No preamble.

Begin Step 1 now.
5 Verify and use

After the boot completes, Pathfinder renders its orientation manifest and declares ready. Start with a small test — a routing question, a service lookup, a scenario request. If anything seems off, you can re-paste the boot mid-session to reset.

Some escalation contacts (the safeguarding lead, the duty clinician system, the Caldicott Guardian) need verifying with the practice — Pathfinder will surface these as boot-time alerts until they are filled in.

About & governance

What Pathfinder is, who maintains it, and what to verify before going live.

⚙
Draft. The governance and version information below relates to the AI / system-prompt version of Pathfinder, which is under separate development. The training tool you're using has its own identity that will be reflected here in a future update.

What this is

Pathfinder is a Care Navigation support tool for Denham & Aysgarth Medical Centre. It helps navigators route patients to the right care — Pharmacy First, self-referral pathways, in-house team members, social prescribing, self-care, or the GP — using the practice's actual team, the local Buckinghamshire services, and structured routing logic.

Pathfinder is a staff tool. It never speaks to patients. It does not diagnose. It does not access EMIS Web or any clinical system. It does not override practice protocols or clinical judgement. Every suggestion is guidance the navigator evaluates against their training and the practice's procedures.

Versions

Governance

Designated maintainer: Emma Woodbridge, Practice Manager. Updates to YAML modules and reference documents route through her.

Reference content reviewers: The Safeguarding Lead, the Complaints Lead (Helena Roome), the Caldicott Guardian / DPO, the Social Prescriber (Hanna Perricone), and the clinical leads.

Maintenance cycle: Monthly check of staff contacts (locums especially), quarterly full review of routing logic and PCN changes, ad-hoc updates when anything changes in the practice or local services.

Verify on day one

Three escalation points need confirming with the practice before taking live calls. Pathfinder surfaces these as boot-time alerts until they're filled in.

The v0.1 caveat

Every reference document in the library is a first draft. They were written quickly on best understanding of NHS frameworks, the practice's structure, and the work itself. They have not yet been reviewed by Denham's clinical or governance leads.

Where they conflict with practice protocol, practice protocol wins. The reviewers named above are the people whose review converts the library from v0.1 study material into a governed practice resource. Until then, treat the library as useful but provisional.

How to update this tool

Open the source of this file in any text editor or LLM-capable interface. Most updates (staff names, contact details, version stamps) only require editing the PATHFINDER_DATA object near the bottom of the file. Reference document content has clearly-marked DOCUMENT N START / DOCUMENT N END insertion points (Phase 2).

Code Lab

Browse, edit, test, and export EMIS quick codes. Starts with 104 shipped codes; add your own.

Type codes and prose on the left. The assembled note renders live on the right — same as it would in an EMIS consultation note. Codes can be on their own line or inline. Unknown codes show in red so you can spot typos.

Your input
Assembled note
Output appears here as you type codes on the left.
0 codes expanded

EMIS Web supports short trigger strings that expand into longer text when typed into a consultation note. You type .pfear, hit space, and the full text appears. The feature is sometimes called auto-text, auto-phrase, or quick-text depending on the EMIS version.

The naming convention

Every code follows the same shape: . + 2-character category prefix + 2-to-4-character mnemonic.

PrefixCategory
.pfPharmacy First (the seven conditions)
.rtRouting to a team member
.srSelf-referral pathways
.euEmergency / urgent (999, 111, A&E, maternity)
.snSafety-netting (general and specific)
.igInformation / leaflet / number given
.pnPatient interaction notes
.pcPatient context flags (carer, FA, housebound)
.adAdmin / escalation
.rfRed flag presentations
.coContinuity (known patient, preferred clinician)

Standalone vs modifier

Standalone codes (e.g. .rtfcp, .eu999) make a complete note on their own. Modifier codes (e.g. .sngen, .pccar) are designed to stack with a standalone — most consultation notes combine 2-3 codes. The Test tab lets you experiment with stacks.

How to add a new code

Click + New code on the Browse tab. The code field must start with ., use one of the 11 category prefixes, and be unique. Pick whether it's standalone or modifier, write the expansion text, add tags. Save. The new code behaves exactly like the shipped codes — searchable, editable, exportable.

Setting them up in EMIS

The exact menu path varies by EMIS Web version and user permissions. The general flow: from a consultation, find the auto-consultation text / auto-phrase / shortcut text setting (often under user settings or personal templates). Add an entry with the trigger code and the expansion text. Test in a new note.

Ask Sandra (Reception Manager) or whoever holds the EMIS Champion role at the practice to show you the first time, rather than guessing the menu path. The mechanism is straightforward once seen.

Exporting

The Export button gives you four formats:

  • CSV — spreadsheet-friendly, for review or backup.
  • EMIS-ready text — tab-separated lines, designed for entering codes one at a time at the EMIS terminal.
  • JSON — full backup of every field. Use this if you want to restore the library on another machine.
  • Printable reference — markdown, all codes alphabetical, suitable for printing as a desk reference.

Resetting

Your edits save automatically to this browser's local storage. If you want to discard everything and return to the 104 shipped codes, the Reset button at the bottom of Export will do that (with confirmation). It cannot be undone.

Maintenance

Codes drift. Phone numbers change. Services rebrand. Once a quarter, re-read the codes containing phone numbers, service names, or clinician names — verify they're still current. Old codes can be edited in place; nothing is locked.

Population Reporting

Reference for EMIS Web's cohort search and reporting tool — concepts, worked examples, and where to learn more.

What it is and where to find it

Population Reporting is EMIS Web's tool for finding patients matching specific clinical criteria. It's how you build recall lists, identify patients needing reviews, run QOF cohorts, support audits, and answer "show me everyone who…" questions.

To open it: EMIS button → Reporting → Population Reporting. (Some practices have it pinned to the Quick Launch Menu or Quick Access Toolbar.) You'll land in a screen with a folder tree on the left and a results pane on the right.

Care Navigator note — Most of what you'll be running is searches built by clinicians, the practice manager, or template providers like Ardens. Knowing the structure means you can read what a search is doing without having to build it from scratch.

The build hierarchy

Every search is built from the same nested structure. Top to bottom:

Folder— where searches are organised (Practice / Ardens / QOF etc.)
└─Search— named cohort with a "Based on" population
   └─Rule— a condition the population must meet
      └─Feature— the thing being checked (Diagnosis, Medication, Patient Details…)
         └─Criteria— the specifics: code selector, value, date range

Each level, plainly

  • Folder — just organisation. Your practice folder, the Ardens folder, the EMIS Library folder, QOF folders, etc.
  • Search — has a name, a description (optional but useful), and a Based on option. The "Based on" is usually Currently registered regular patients, but it can also be another search's results — that's the parent/child mechanism.
  • Rule — clicking "Click here to begin adding a Rule" opens the Feature Builder. A search can have multiple rules; they combine with AND by default.
  • Feature — what category of thing you're checking. Examples: Patient Details (age, sex), Diagnosis (clinical codes), Medication (drugs), Investigation (test results), Online Users, Appointments, Consultations.
  • Criteria — the specifics. For a clinical feature, that's the SNOMED CT code(s), the value range (e.g. cholesterol > 9), and the date constraint (e.g. in last 12 months).

The parent / child pattern

Two ways to refine a cohort. They look similar but work differently.

Child search (cohort-on-cohort)

You create a parent search — say, "All patients over 30". When you create a second search, instead of "Based on currently registered patients" you pick "Use an existing search's results" and point at the parent. Now the child search starts from the parent's output and refines further — "of those over 30, find ones with cholesterol > 9.0". You can chain this several levels deep, and each link in the chain stays maintainable as its own search.

Best for: cohort building you want to reuse. The parent ("over 30") might be useful in five different child searches across different clinical areas.

Linked features (within one rule)

Inside a single rule, you can link one feature to another. Example: feature 1 is "Diagnosis of diabetes", feature 2 is "HbA1c value > 58 mmol/mol" linked to feature 1. The link says "the test must be associated with the diagnosis", or you can leave it unlinked and just have both as criteria the patient must meet.

Linked features also support date relationships — feature 1 must be before feature 2, or within 12 months of feature 2.

Best for: clinical logic that belongs together in one place — "diagnosis with associated test result" reads cleanly as one rule.

Quick rule of thumb — if you find yourself rebuilding the same cohort definition in multiple searches, make it a parent search instead. If two conditions only make sense together, keep them as linked features in one rule.

Search vs Report

Searches find patients. Reports format what you get back.

  • Search alone — gives you a list of patients matching the criteria. You can open this and see names. Useful for working through a recall list one by one.
  • List Report — built on a search. Lets you choose which columns appear and in what order (e.g. patient name, DOB, last HbA1c value, last HbA1c date). Best for action-ready outputs you'll print or export.
  • Aggregate Report — built on a search. Gives counts and groupings rather than individual patients (e.g. "how many in each age band", "count by GP"). Best for audit and reporting upwards.

Running and scheduling

To run a search: highlight it and click Run on the ribbon, or right-click and pick Run. The patient count updates in the Population Count column.

You can also schedule searches to run automatically — right-click → Properties → Schedule tab. Weekly is common for recall lists; daily for things needing tight follow-up.

Search outputs can be exported to CSV individually (not all at once) — useful for any work that needs to leave EMIS, though always check what the practice policy says about exporting patient-identifiable data before doing so.

Six common Care Navigator cohorts. Each one is a recipe — the steps you'd click through in EMIS Population Reporting. Codes given are SNOMED CT concept names; the exact code lookup will be the same regardless of practice.

1
Patients due flu vaccination
Find patients eligible for a seasonal flu jab who haven't had one this season.
›
  1. Parent search: Create a new Search in your practice folder. Name: Flu eligible — registered. Based on: Currently registered regular patients.
  2. Rule 1: Add a rule. Feature: Patient Details → Criteria: Age ≥ 65 OR (Age 6 months to under 65 AND in clinical risk group). Combine with OR.
  3. Child search: Create a second search. Name: Flu eligible — no jab this season. Based on: Use an existing search's results → select the parent.
  4. Rule: Feature: Clinical codes → Code selector: Seasonal influenza vaccination (and "Influenza vaccination given by other healthcare provider"). Criteria: No record, Date range: this season (e.g. from 1 September).
  5. Output: Build a List Report on the child search. Columns: Patient name, DOB, Usual GP, Phone number, Last flu vaccination date.
Note — Most practices have this pre-built in Ardens or the EMIS Library. Check there first; you'll find a more thoroughly built version than this skeleton.
2
Patients with diabetes overdue HbA1c
Diabetic patients who haven't had an HbA1c test in the last 12 months.
›
  1. Parent search: Name: Diabetic register. Based on: Currently registered patients. Rule — Feature: Diagnosis → Code: Diabetes mellitus (Type 1 OR Type 2). Criteria: Has record (any date).
  2. Child search: Name: Diabetes — no HbA1c 12 months. Based on: Use the parent's results.
  3. Rule: Feature: Investigation → Code selector: HbA1c level. Criteria: No record, Date range: within last 12 months.
  4. Output: List Report with patient name, DOB, last HbA1c date (will be blank or >12 months old), usual GP. Sort by last test date so the longest overdue surface first.
QOF tip — the QOF target is HbA1c ≤ 58 mmol/mol within the year. Practice may already have an Ardens or QOF library version doing this with the exact target value built in.
3
Patients on long-term opioids needing review
Repeat opioid prescriptions with no opioid review in the last 6 months.
›
  1. Parent search: Name: On long-term opioids. Rule — Feature: Medication → Code selector: Opioid analgesics drug class (codeine, tramadol, morphine, oxycodone, fentanyl, buprenorphine etc.). Criteria: Repeat issued, two or more issues in last 6 months.
  2. Child search: Name: Opioids — no review 6 months. Based on: parent results.
  3. Rule: Feature: Clinical codes → Code: Medication review (or Opioid review if practice uses a specific code). Criteria: No record in last 6 months.
  4. Output: List Report. Columns: name, DOB, drug name, last review date, usual GP. Aggregate Report can also break this down by drug class for chronic pain audits.
Care Navigator action — this typically generates a list for booking medication review appointments. Confirm with the duty GP or PM whether navigator-initiated booking is the practice's process here, or whether the clinical pharmacist (Jaspreet, Pinali, or Farah) reviews the list first.
4
Patients 75+ overdue medication review
Older patients on repeat medication with no review in 12 months.
›
  1. Parent search: Name: Aged 75+ on repeats. Rule 1 — Patient Details: Age ≥ 75. Rule 2 — Medication: any repeat medication issued in the last 6 months.
  2. Child search: Name: 75+ on repeats — no review 12 months. Based on: parent.
  3. Rule: Clinical code Medication review: No record in last 12 months.
  4. Output: List Report with name, DOB, count of repeat items, last review date, usual GP. Use to populate batch booking lists for medication reviews.
5
Hypertensive patients with no recent BP
Patients with hypertension diagnosis and no BP reading in last 6 months.
›
  1. Parent search: Name: Hypertension register. Rule — Diagnosis: Hypertension (or Essential hypertension). Criteria: Has record, any date.
  2. Child search: Name: HTN — no BP 6 months. Based on parent.
  3. Rule: Feature: Investigation → Code selectors: Blood pressure reading, Home blood pressure reading, Ambulatory blood pressure recording. Criteria: No record in last 6 months.
  4. Output: List Report. Add last BP value and date columns so the clinician can see how stale the data is at a glance.
Tip — include home and ambulatory BP code variants. Many hypertensive patients monitor at home and the readings may come in by letter / Accurx — if the search only checks in-clinic BP it'll over-call patients who are actually being monitored.
6
Patients over 30 with cholesterol over 9.0
Familial hypercholesterolaemia (FH) screening pattern — the canonical training example.
›
  1. Parent search: Name: Patients aged 30+. Rule — Patient Details: Age ≥ 30.
  2. Child search: Name: Aged 30+, cholesterol > 9. Based on: parent.
  3. Rule: Feature: Investigation → Code selector: Serum cholesterol (and Serum total cholesterol). Criteria: Has record, Value > 9.0 mmol/L. Add a date range if you only want recent readings (e.g. last 5 years).
  4. Output: List Report with name, DOB, cholesterol value, test date, usual GP. Sort by value descending — highest readings first.
Clinical context — the NICE FH guideline recommends specialist review for adults with total cholesterol > 7.5 mmol/L plus family history, or > 9.0 alone. A list like this would normally go to a GP for review rather than triggering navigator action directly. Always escalate clinically significant findings — never act unilaterally on results data.

NHS-aligned training and reference

Pick whichever format fits — video, written guide, or hands-on practice library. These are the ones most likely to match what Denham actually uses.

EMIS NUG — Introduction to Searches and Reports
National User Group video walkthrough using a hypertension audit example. Covers Search, Aggregate Report, and List Report tools end-to-end.
emisnug.org.uk/introduction-searches-and-reports
Ardens Academy
Free training including "Getting Started with Ardens EMIS → Searches and Reports". If the practice uses Ardens templates (most do), this is the most directly applicable resource.
support-ew.ardens.org.uk
MLCSU Academy
Step-by-step user guides for specific searches (online services audit, recall lists, QOF cohorts). Written, scannable, NHS-published.
academy.midlandsandlancashirecsu.nhs.uk
EMISNow (official knowledge base)
EMIS's own support documentation. Most authoritative source for current UI specifics. Requires login.
emisnow.com
DNA Insight — Creating Searches PDF
Comprehensive written reference covering search creation, linked features, dates, and counts. Good as a desk reference if you prefer reading.
dnainsight.co.uk (PDF)
Practice resources
Ask Sandra or Emma whether there's a folder of existing searches the practice has built up. Inheriting maintained searches is faster than re-building.
Internal — Denham PM / Reception Mgr

Pathfinder caveat

Pathfinder is a Care Navigator training and reference tool. It is not connected to EMIS Web, holds no patient data, and cannot run searches. Everything in this view describes the structure and pattern of Population Reporting so you can read existing searches, understand what's being asked of you, and have informed conversations with the practice manager or clinicians who build them.

Anything destined for actual EMIS use — running searches, scheduling, exports — happens in the live system under practice governance. Confirm RBAC permissions and practice policy before running or modifying searches.

EMIS Shortcuts

Keyboard shortcuts for EMIS Web and general Windows actions. Verify EMIS-specific ones against your build — they can vary by version and configuration.

Add a shortcut
Keys Action Category Source
Source key: Verified = confirmed at Denham · Verify locally = documented for EMIS Web but check at your build · General = standard Windows · Custom = added by you

Task Cards

"How do I…" recipes for the everyday EMIS actions you'll meet in your first weeks. Verify each one against your build and tag it when confirmed.

EMIS Glossary

Terms, acronyms, and jargon you'll hear in your first weeks. Click any related term to jump to it.

EMIS Drill

Macros

Suggested macro designs — bundles of consultation type + SNOMED codes + free-text scaffold. To take to Emma for configuration in the live EMIS system at practice level.

How macros differ from Quick Codes — Quick Codes (Code Lab) are trigger-text expanders: type .pfear, space, and the stock phrase drops in. Macros are structured bundles: one action sets a consultation type, fires several SNOMED codes at once, and inserts a free-text scaffold with placeholders. Macros are configured at practice level in EMIS (talk to Emma); this view holds the designs.
·

Walk a call

A step-by-step routing aid. Click what you're hearing as the patient describes it — the tool narrows the candidate routes as you go. Use it live, or use it to practise.

Walk log

Saved to this browser only. Export for review or practice notes.

Symptom basket

The signal vocabulary builder. Where the canvas teaches you to recognise calls, the basket teaches you how the engine thinks. Combine signals freely. Click any signal to read what it means. Watch the route shift as you add or remove signals — that’s the cascade in action.

Three ways to use this
  • Pick a worked example below to load a curated signal combination. Each one demonstrates a specific routing principle. Add or remove signals to see how the route changes.
  • Build your own combination by searching or browsing signals. Try patterns from calls you've taken or scenarios you're not sure about.
  • Read every signal you add. Click the chip in the basket to see what it means and what it does to routing. This is where the cascade becomes intuitive.
Worked examples

Pre-built signal combinations that demonstrate the most important routing patterns. Load one, then try modifying it to see how the engine responds.

Current basket
No signals yet — load a worked example above, search below, or pick a complaint to start.
Or browse by category:
Add signals to see candidate routes.

Mistakes journal

Scenarios from the Training canvas where your routing differed from the engine. Each one is here to be revisited — re-read the teaching note, redo the scenario, learn the pattern. Once you get it right, it falls off the list.

Mistakes journal is the most useful page in Pathfinder. The scenarios you got wrong are more instructive than the ones you got right. Revisit weekly — even a quick re-read of the teaching note often surfaces what you missed.

Training canvas

The practice surface. Pick a scenario, drag the signals you'd capture from the call onto the patient card, then choose where you'd route. The engine shows what it recommends and where your routing differed. A few minutes a day is enough — repeated reps are how the cascade becomes intuitive.

No scenario loaded — pick one to begin.

Pick a scenario above to begin a practice session.

Call records

EMIS-paste-ready summaries generated from your walks. Reviewable, editable, exportable. Stays on this device — no patient-identifiable detail is generated automatically.

No call records yet.

When you complete a walk, the route, signals, and safety-net are auto-formatted into an EMIS-paste-ready record that lands here.

Edit record

All routes

Every route a Care Navigator can use at Denham. Click any route for the full scope, eligibility, contact details, what to say, and common misroutes.

What is Pathfinder?

An interactive training manual for Care Navigators at Denham & Aysgarth Medical Centre. Use it to learn the cascade, practise the patterns, build confidence over time.

What it is

Pathfinder is primarily an interactive training manual for Care Navigators — a place to practise scenarios, learn the routes, and build the patterns that make calls go well. It can also be used live during a call when something is genuinely unclear, but that should become rarer over time. A navigator who graduates out of needing it has used it well.

Three things Pathfinder isn't, worth saying clearly:

  • It does not speak to patients directly. You speak to patients. Pathfinder is for you, not them.
  • It does not diagnose. Clinical decisions belong to clinicians. Pathfinder helps you reach the right clinician, faster.
  • It does not override practice protocols. If Denham handles something a specific way, that takes priority.
The four rules Pathfinder operates by
1
Support, never replace. Pathfinder assists; it does not diagnose, override protocols, or substitute for clinical judgement. When in doubt — escalate to a clinician.
2
Empower the patient through the navigator. A GP appointment is one option among many. Pharmacy First, self-referral, social prescribing, self-care — all empower patients to access the right care faster.
3
Local first. Suggestions are grounded in what's actually available to Denham patients — this practice, this PCN, this area. Generic NHS guidance comes second to local knowledge.
4
Governed by the practice. Emma (Practice Manager) maintains the rule set, the route content, and the scope. If you spot something wrong — flag it in the walker, or tell Emma.
Where to start

Pathfinder is primarily an interactive training manual. Spend time with it in spare moments and you'll need it less when you're on calls. A navigator who outgrows it has used it well.

The Glossary and Mistakes journal work passively — terms inside route pages link out to glossary definitions, and the journal collects scenarios you've got wrong so you can revisit them. Call records is for live-call use, if you choose to use it.

Things worth knowing
  • The right care, right person, right time. That's the motto. Sometimes that's a GP. Often it isn't.
  • Red flags hard-stop the walker. If a 999-level signal is added, the tool jumps you straight to the recommendation. Don't keep gathering signals when escalation is clear.
  • Adjacents matter. Many calls have two layers — the medical surface and the social underneath. The walker surfaces both. Offering the adjacent is often where the real care happens.
  • Nothing leaves your device. Pathfinder doesn't store patient details. Call Records are local to your browser. EMIS notes are generated without patient identifiers — you add those when you paste.
  • You can flag the engine. If a walk produced the wrong recommendation, mark it with the flag button. Emma reviews flagged walks — that's how the rules improve.

You can reopen this anytime via the ? button in the topbar (next to the theme toggle). Press ? on the keyboard for the shortcuts overlay.
